title: Macramé sunburst wall art
subtitle: A circular sunburst piece on a metal ring, approximately 40 cm diameter, with radiating square knots and double half-hitches
excerpt: A circular macramé sunburst made on a 20 cm metal ring. All cords are mounted at the centre of the ring with lark's head knots and worked outward in radiating lines of square knots and double half-hitches. The outermost cords form the sunray fringe. Materials: 3 mm natural cotton cord, 40 cords at 120 cm each.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from the mid-20th-century macramé revival. Circular ring-mounted macramé pieces with radiating cord structures are a standard pattern type in the revival tradition. Square knots, double half-hitches, and lark's head mounts are documented in Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work, Cornell Maritime Press, 1939.

glossaryTerms.definition: A symmetrical four-cord knot built from two opposing half-knots. In the sunburst piece, square knots are tied in radiating pairs working outward from the central ring, building up the inner knotted disc from which the ray fringe extends.

A simple two-looped mount knot used to attach cords to a ring or holding cord. The cord is folded in half and both ends are pulled through the loop at the fold. In the sunburst, all cords are mounted on the metal ring at the centre using lark's head knots.

A knot made by looping a working cord twice around a holding cord. Rows of double half hitches form the circular rings of the sunburst pattern, worked around the piece horizontally as the work grows outward from the central mount.

content.text: The macramé sunburst is worked outward from a small central metal ring. All 40 cords are mounted on the ring with
lark's head knots
, giving 80 working ends radiating outward.
Square knots
and
double half-hitches
build up the disc in rings, with the outermost cords left as fringe to form the sunrays. Finished size: approximately 40 cm diameter including fringe.
Cord cutting guide
Cut 40 cords at 120 cm each in 3 mm natural cotton cord.
Fold each cord in half before mounting: each lark's head mount gives two working ends at 60 cm, enough for the knotted disc section plus approximately 10 to 15 cm of fringe.
Key knots used

definition.caption: Lark's head knot — fold one cord in half and mount both ends through the loop onto the central ring
Square knot — two working cords around two filler cords; forms the inner disc of the sunburst

content.text: Method
Step 1: Mount cords on the central ring

Clip the ring to your macramé board at a comfortable working height using T-pins through a short cord looped around the ring. Mount all 40 cords with lark's head knots, spacing them evenly around the ring. On a standard 5 to 6 cm ring, spacing 40 mounts will be close together; press each mount toward its neighbour as you work to keep the ring evenly covered.
Step 2: First ring of square knots

Group the 80 working ends into groups of 4 (20 groups). Tie a square knot in each group, positioned approximately 2 to 3 cm from the central ring. The knots will form the first ring of the disc. Because the cords radiate outward, each square knot group naturally spreads slightly as you work outward from the centre.
Step 3: Double half-hitch ring

Take one outer cord from each adjacent square knot group and loop a separate short cord around the work at approximately 5 cm from the centre to act as a holding cord. Tie a double half hitch onto this holding cord with each working cord. This forms a clean circular line around the inner disc edge.
Step 4: Second and third rings of square knots

Work a second ring of alternating square knots at approximately 7 cm radius, then a third ring at approximately 10 cm radius. Each ring uses the alternating square knot offset: take 2 cords from each adjacent group and 2 from the other side to form new groups of 4 for each successive ring. As the circumference grows outward, the groups may need to be split further to avoid cords crossing.
Step 5: Outer fringe edge

Leave the remaining cord lengths beyond the last knot ring as the sunray fringe. Separate each cord individually and trim to your planned ray length, all at the same length for a clean circular silhouette. The fringe should extend approximately 8 to 10 cm beyond the last knotted ring. Untwist the cut cord ends and brush lightly to separate the fibres into a fluffy ray texture.
Finishing

Weave all loose knot tails back into the work from behind using a blunt darning needle. If the disc is not lying flat, mist it lightly with water and pin it flat on a board to dry. The ring can be hung directly through the top of the mount ring.
